speaker: interviewer
text: September 1995. So Ruth, your-- your husband died. You-- you don't have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what has happened to you in the-- in the-- in the time since your husband's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I was-- well, you can call it I was lucky. I had a very good bank manager, and I told him I wanted to go away because I was so terribly distraught that I thought I'd go see whatever rest of my family was alive. And I had to worry about my house, and I also had some business running.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought I need somebody reliable. And he said, gee, I have somebody. I have somebody, as it was, after his divorce and also very down, and I said, all right, you know what you can do? Let him ring me, and we spoke of it on the phone, and I invited him for dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw he is clean person, an honest person, I thought an intelligent man, and-- and he was very unhappy because he was here in a room and always food, but not very pleasant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said look, I go-- I want to go over this. And I need somebody for my place, somebody reliable. Would you take over? And I explained for the business part, which I had to do. I just left everything to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't make the wrong judgement because when I came back, we decided that he stays by me in the room, we transfer our surgery. And he is a very good friend, very, very reliable. He showed that in my hardest days, when I was so terribly sick, twice or three times already, he stood by my side. He never missed coming and helping. And whatever is in life, he protects me. So I couldn't wish for anything better. So yeah, I won twice the lottery, not once.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he's been a really wonderful, wonderful friend to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: An excellent friend, very, very good. Hasn't want me to have a worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, when you-- when you look back on the experiences that-- that you have lived through, particularly during the war, can you think back to the time-- was there any time that stood out as being the-- the-- the worst time that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The worst time was the time when they sent my late father to the transport because I had the feeling that I'll never see him again. And the second, when I lost-- I knew I lost my mother in Auschwitz, and third, when I heard I lost my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was these-- the loss of the-- the people who you loved so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the family. Exactly. That-- that was my worst. Whatever happened directly to me, I took, but that was something which destroyed me, which destroyed my inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned, and we discussed before, your belief that you would survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you did you ever lose that belief? Did you ever think that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I always had a very strong will to live. And I just couldn't think that I wouldn't make it. I always, I never-- well, as I told you after the war, the experience with-- with this Russian, that I thought, yes. That is--

speaker: interviewer
text: You thought you were going to die then? You thought he'd rape you and kill you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the only moment. I thought there is no way out of it. But otherwise, no, I thought always I-- I make it. I make it. I make it. It was the time when I was sort of-- I didn't know where to go when-- when Wagner refused me. And we really didn't have anywhere to go. I was hungry. I stole carrots from the field. Pulled them-- pulled them out and ate them. That was a very hard time. And there, I nearly thought, well maybe, maybe that will be it. But I had always a strong will because it was-- it was what we say chutzpah to go a second time back to this German fellow. And still, I didn't see another way I had to do it. And I was, at that time, still I was a very shy person. I wasn't-- you know, some people, they have very thick skin. I didn't have that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it was very, very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I learned to have it, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you survived almost impossible odds.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is exactly right. That is-- so I-- I always say I meant to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you why you think you have survived? Why did you? Was it this--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think it is-- it was a higher force. It was absolutely God wanted me to survive. Because I was in so much danger, surrounded with so many enemies, even when I was not in the camp, when I lived on my Aryan paper. But I think it is a higher force which wanted me to survive. And I really survived to live with my husband together. That was a reward for all the bad things I experienced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Ruth I want to thank you very, very much for-- for sharing that extraordinary story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're most welcome. I feel before I die I have to let it know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you have done a-- a-- a wonderful thing in-- in-- in doing this and thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're most welcome, sweetie. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture is very valuable for me. First of all, it is a photo of my dad. Then it shows the love he had for me. And it went with me to the concentration camp. It went with me when I lived on the Aryan papers, and it is still with me. It is just unfortunately, not very clear anymore, but it must be 73 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year do you think this could have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think it might have been '22 or '23, because you see on me, I am fairly tiny.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ruth, tell me who are the people here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is my mother, my brother, and myself. We were on holidays in Czechoslovakia in [? Johannesburg. ?] I remember that. And I got it. I didn't have it. I got it back from an aunt of mine who said it belongs more to you than to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year do you think this might have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have on the back I was 10 years of age, that means 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a synagogue in Katowice, where my parents were married and where I was shipped. And this synagogue was standing to 1939 until the Germans came in and was completely destroyed. It was-- to see that, for me, when I came back into-- into Katowice was same as a piece of me was destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who are the people here?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my mother and my grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's mother or your father's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother's mother, my grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Ruth, who are the people here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here is my father and my brother. And according to that, it must have been 1934, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where the photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Tell me about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my only brother, whom I dearly loved and whom I lost so tragically.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in the ghetto. He must have used it for some documents because it looks like a passport photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it would have been taken in which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '42 it was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which ghetto was it taken in just--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Chanov. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Ruth, tell me what this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a document, which verified that I worked in Germany, and with this document, I was able to get work. And it was very important for me because it helped me in my Aryan life, in my life outside the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was given to you by the woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was given to me by a German woman, who distributed actually these books, these working books. But I didn't have any other papers and she was good enough with help. What I told her, that I come from Hamburg and everything was destroyed. She was good enough. She said, I give you a working book. And so that way, I got it. And after the war, I met a girl who worked at the same township in [GERMAN] at the post office. And she got sort of the same way her arbeitsbuch, her working book and through the same lady. And she said, you know, that-- that was a lady who had a Jewish husband and whenever she could help Jewish people, she don't-- didn't miss out. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your passport to life, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Ruth, tell me about this here.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the name you see there, [? Veronisova ?] [? Shanovska ?] was the name under which I lived and worked. It was the name taken from my Polish teacher, not to forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. All right, look at the thing for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, the writing-- so it's got the-- the-- the-- the place, which is the generale government, and then it's got--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where I come from. That says general government. And then date of birth is the 14th January '20, which is my real birth date, and then [INAUDIBLE], Pinczow, which was a little township completely destroyed before my eyes during the war when the Germans come back. Therefore, I picked on that because it was hard to--

speaker: interviewer
text: To verify, and that's exactly what I want you to tell me. And then what's the next line here?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next line--

speaker: interviewer
text: This one here. [? Starachowice, ?] yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Starachowice. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was your state [INAUDIBLE]. Let's wait for Kim to let me-- Ruth, tell me about these pages in your arbeitsbuch.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first page is [NON-ENGLISH], that means country I come from. And they wrote it as general governement. That means they were the occupiers there of this Polish part. And then it says geburtsdatum, that means the date of birth, which I gave as my normal, well, natural birth date. It is the 14th of January, 1920. And then they say [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where I was born. And I gave Pinczow, which is a very little town where I was at the beginning of the war, and I saw it completely destroyed to the last. And then it-- it-- [NON-ENGLISH]. That means to what nation I belong, and they wrote stateless. And then it says to what nation I belong, second time. That is Polish. They wrote I am a Pole. And then is-- was I-- I'm not married. That is the last thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the photo, of course, is your photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo is my photograph and my sad face speaks for itself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, what does this say here?

speaker: interviewee
text: It says here that I was working in Oels at [INAUDIBLE] on-- on-- on the railway station, actually. It was a railway restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Ruth, tell me about this picture here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it meant a lot of me. I was younger. I was very poor but very happy. That what it isn't-- I-- I saw it. I won the lottery with my husband, what I still am thinking today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's you and your husband, and it was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Katowice, my birthplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which year would this be?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what is this picture here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here it is at our arrival in Australia. That was the 3rd of December, '49. And that is taken with the two people, George and Lotti Galefsky, which were our sponsors to Australia. And, well, it was just we were very happy to be here and I think our faces show it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Ruth, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, we were on a holiday in Surfers Paradise. And I think when you see our happy faces, they speak for themselves. Always happy to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when would this have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't need it anymore, because I have done the-- I think '65.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Jan, and we made a boat trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great. Thank you. Ruth, are you going to do the introductions for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that is Arthur Corio, my good friend, my good companion, my everything. He's my protector, and I'm very happy to be together with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Arthur, is there something that you might want to say, now that you're here on-- on-- on tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'd-- I'd like to say that we've-- we've been together for 21 years. And well, Ruth said it a few seconds ago-- I would like to think I'm a good friend, confidant, and companion. And I try to make every day a happy day for Ruth, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because of the sadness of which she's had to sort of face the past. We don't talk much about it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] simply because the memories are painful. And I'm grateful that she's had this opportunity to put this down as a matter of record because I think it will be a great comfort of-- for her to know that-- that her people have [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not been on this Earth in vain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's a-- it's a sad-- a sad chapter in-- in-- in-- in history which I hope will-- will, because of this record, will never be repeated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And Ruth to finish, is there something that you would like to conclude with, something that you wish to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I wish to say, maybe through our suffering the-- Israel was came to nationhood and we contributed practically, not willingly, but it happened. It is there, and hopefully, it will protect us and see that such Holocaust wouldn't be repeated. And all my wish is Israel high. That is what-- I'm Israel high. That is what I wish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, and thank you for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you that I could, and it was a pleasure.
